Member-only story

The biggest mistake wasn’t misunderstanding the keyword. It was assuming JavaScript cared who owned a function.

7 min read
1 hour ago

--

There are a few JavaScript concepts that almost every developer claims to understand — until production proves otherwise.
this is one of them.
Ask ten developers how this works and you'll probably hear ten different explanations.
“It refers to the object.”
“It depends on where the function is defined.”
“It’s whatever is before the dot.”
“It’s the current object.”
Every one of those explanations works… until it doesn’t.
For years, I thought I understood this. My code usually behaved the way I expected. Then I started writing larger applications, debugging event handlers, reviewing teammate code, mixing callbacks with asynchronous APIs, and working more deeply with Node.js and React.
That’s when I realized something uncomfortable:
I wasn’t using this correctly.
I was simply getting lucky.
The real lesson wasn’t another rule to memorize. It was replacing the mental model I’d carried for…
